section_plan.## 研究背景与问题: adversarial transferability gap between CNNs and ViTs; why equal-weight ensemble fails
section_plan.## 方法主线: AGM (adaptive gradient modulation) + DRF (disparity-reduced filter) — detailed mechanism flow
section_plan.## 实验设计: datasets, models, baselines, hyperparams, evaluation protocol
section_plan.## 核心实验结果: main black-box ASR tables, ablation studies, defense-attack results
section_plan.## 讨论与局限: what the paper does and does not prove; limitations
section_plan.## 原文摘要翻译: faithful Chinese translation of original abstract

central_claims.claim: Equal-weight fusion in ensemble attacks smooths out transferable information, causing under-optimized results
central_claims.supporting_evidence: Fig. 2 cosine similarity heatmap shows gradient dissimilarity across architectures; Table 4 shows simple ensemble baseline (27.29%) vs AdaEA (44.78%)
central_claims.what_it_proves: There exists gradient disparity across diverse architectures, and naive averaging loses individual model strengths
central_claims.what_it_does_not_prove: Does not prove that the loss of transferable info is the sole cause; other factors like optimization landscape may contribute

central_claims.claim: AGM adaptively weights surrogate models by their adversarial ratio, amplifying transferable information
central_claims.supporting_evidence: Table 4: +AGM improves over baseline by 12.5% average ASR; the adversarial ratio exploits cross-model attack performance as a proxy for transferability
central_claims.what_it_proves: Adaptive weighting based on inter-model attack performance is effective
central_claims.what_it_does_not_prove: The adversarial ratio is the optimal proxy; no comparison with alternative transferability metrics

central_claims.claim: DRF synchronizes gradient update direction by filtering out disparity components, mitigating overfitting
central_claims.supporting_evidence: Table 4: +DRF improves ViT transfer from 23.94% to 47.57%; qualitative analysis in supplementary material
central_claims.what_it_proves: Filtering gradient disparity helps cross-architecture transfer
central_claims.what_it_does_not_prove: The binarization threshold η=-0.3 is universally optimal; sensitivity analysis only covers CIFAR-10

central_claims.claim: AdaEA can be plugged into existing transfer-based attacks and boosts them consistently
central_claims.supporting_evidence: Table 2: AdaEA boosts FGSM (+24.12%), I-FGSM (+18.27%), MI-FGSM (+17.74%), DI²-FGSM (+7.88%) over baseline Ens
central_claims.what_it_proves: AdaEA is a versatile ensemble wrapper, not a standalone attack
central_claims.what_it_does_not_prove: Effectiveness on attacks beyond those tested (e.g., targeted attacks, patch attacks)

claim_boundaries: All experiments use l∞ constraint with ε=8/255; transferability under other threat models (l2, l0) is not studied
Surrogate models are all standardly trained; adversarial-trained surrogates not explored
ImageNet evaluation uses only a subset of models; statistical significance tests not reported
No evaluation on object detection or segmentation downstream tasks
negative_or_limiting_results: SVRE degrades significantly under DI²-FGSM (Ens: 72.44% → SVRE: 33.49%, -38.95%), while AdaEA improves it (+7.88%)
When only CNNs are surrogate models, attack on ViTs remains weak (~10-13%), suggesting architecture gap is not fully bridged
AdaEA's advantage shrinks on ImageNet vs CIFAR (+2.98% vs +18.53%), possibly due to higher baseline difficulty

mechanism_result_map.mechanism: AGM: adversarial ratio ρ_i measures how well adversarial examples from model i attack other models
mechanism_result_map.result_explained: Table 4 CNN ASR: baseline 29.96% → +AGM 39.48%; the adaptive weighting prevents dominant models from suppressing transferable signals
mechanism_result_map.design_choice: Using logit-based cross-entropy loss for adversarial ratio computation (Eq. 3-4), weight scale β=10

mechanism_result_map.mechanism: DRF: cosine similarity-based disparity map binarized at threshold η
mechanism_result_map.result_explained: Table 4 ViT ASR: baseline 23.94% → +DRF 47.57%; the filter removes gradient components where models disagree, synchronizing the update direction
mechanism_result_map.design_choice: Channel-wise cosine similarity (Eq. 6), element-wise binary mask (Eq. 7), η=-0.3

mechanism_result_map.result_explained: Table 1: AdaEA achieves 45.09% (CIFAR-10), 71.10% (CIFAR-100), 49.36% (ImageNet) average ASR, consistently exceeding Ens and SVRE
mechanism_result_map.design_choice: AGM modulates weight then DRF filters disparity; order matters (AGM first, then DRF applied to weighted ensemble)

comparative_positioning.vs_prior: Ens (Liu et al. 2017) uses equal-weight average; SVRE (Xiong et al. 2022) reduces variance but still treats all models uniformly
comparative_positioning.difference: AdaEA does not treat models uniformly — it adaptively modulates weight based on transferability contribution (AGM) and synchronizes gradient direction (DRF)
comparative_positioning.interpretation_change: The ensemble problem is reframed from variance reduction to information amplification — each model's contribution is actively shaped rather than passively averaged

comparative_positioning.vs_prior: Transfer-based methods (MI-FGSM, DI²-FGSM) focus on single-surrogate perturbation crafting
comparative_positioning.difference: AdaEA operates at the ensemble level and is orthogonal — it can wrap any gradient-based attack on multiple surrogates
comparative_positioning.interpretation_change: Ensemble diversity is not just about model count; how you fuse their signals matters as much as which models you include

reuse_takeaways: AGM + DRF is a drop-in ensemble wrapper for any iterative gradient-based attack (I-FGSM, MI-FGSM, DI²-FGSM, etc.)
The adversarial ratio defined in Eq. (3-4) can serve as a general transferability diagnostic for ensemble selection
DRF's cosine similarity-based disparity filtering may generalize to other multi-model optimization problems beyond adversarial attacks
When building an ensemble for black-box attack, including both CNN and ViT surrogates is critical for cross-architecture transfer
followup_questions: Can AGM's adversarial ratio be computed more efficiently without O(K²) forward passes?
Does AdaEA help targeted attacks and physical-world attacks?
Can the DRF threshold η be learned or scheduled rather than fixed?
How does AdaEA perform with adversarially trained surrogates?
Can AdaEA's weighting scheme be interpreted as attention over surrogate models, and does it reveal which models contribute most to transferability?
